coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of ultrathin carbon dots-coated iron oxide nanocubes decorated with silver nanoparticles and their excellent catalytic properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yali
Tang, Dong
Zhang, Lilan
Li, Bo
Iqbal, Anam
Liu, Weisheng
Qin, Wenwu
coredata.dc:description: A facile ultrasonic method has been successfully developed for the fabrication of multifunctional Fe3O4@carbon dot/Ag (Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag) nanocubes (NCs), and the resulting materials are well characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and fluorescence measurements. The Ag nanoparticles (NPs) are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of Fe3O4@C-dot, while maintaining the shape and the size of the core-shell Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. In addition, its catalytic activities are evaluated by measuring the reduction of p-nitroaniline (p-NA) and crystal violet (CV), and the composite materials exhibit excellent catalytic activity towards reduction of p-NA and CV dye, which is superior to most reported catalysts. The good catalytic performance of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs may be attributed to the specific characteristics of its nanostructure and the synergistic effect on the delivery of electrons between Ag NPs and Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. Furthermore, the as-prepared catalysts also show good activity for the reduction of other nitrobenzene analogs. The effect of solvent and reducing agent was also studied on the catalytic activity of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. Most importantly, the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag catalyst shows excellent recycling stabilities, which can be potentially applied in the fields of catalysis and green chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Magnetic nanocubes
Silver nanoparticles
Ultrasonic method
Catalytic property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the reduction of p-nitroaniline (p-NA)
General procedure for the reduction of crystal violet (CV) dye
Potential mechanism for the reduction reaction
Comparison for the reduction of p-NA with different catalysts
Effects of solvent and reducing agent on catalytic activity
Comparison for the reduction of CV dye with different catalysts

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of as-prepared C-dots. (Inset: Size distribution histogram obtained from TEM measurements).
TEM images of (a, b and c) Fe3O4 NCs and (d, e and f) Fe3O4@C-dot NCs with different magnifications.
TEM images of (a, b and c) Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs with different magnifications. (d) HRTEM image of the Ag crystal structure.
HAADF-STEM and elemental mapping images of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs.
XRD patterns of (a) C-dots, (b) Fe3O4 NCs, (c) Fe3O4@C-dot NCs and (d) Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Fe 2p and (e) Ag 3d for the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs.
Magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C-dot and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. The inset pattern shows the magnetic separation of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs from aqueous solution.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the p-NA and NaBH4 aqueous solution (without catalyst). Time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra for the reduction of p-AP with NaBH4 in the presence of (b) C-dots, (c) Fe3O4@C-dot NCs, (d) bare Ag NPs, (e) Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. (h) Plots of ln(C/C0) versus reaction time for the catalytic reduction of p-AP with different catalysts.
Time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra for the reduction of CV with NaBH4 in the presence of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs.

(a) The reusability of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag catalysts for the reduction of p-NA and CV dye. (b) TEM image of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs after the reduction reactions. (c) XRD patterns of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs before and after the reaction.
Illustration of the catalytic mechanism for the reduction of p-NA (as an example) by the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs.
Comparison of various catalysts with the present work for the reduction of p-NA.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of ultrathin carbon dots-coated iron oxide nanocubes decorated with silver nanoparticles and their excellent catalytic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lishui University, Lishui City 323000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile ultrasonic method has been successfully developed for the fabrication of multifunctional Fe3O4@carbon dot/Ag (Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag) nanocubes (NCs), and the resulting materials are well characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and fluorescence measurements. The Ag nanoparticles (NPs) are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of Fe3O4@C-dot, while maintaining the shape and the size of the core-shell Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. In addition, its catalytic activities are evaluated by measuring the reduction of p-nitroaniline (p-NA) and crystal violet (CV), and the composite materials exhibit excellent catalytic activity towards reduction of p-NA and CV dye, which is superior to most reported catalysts. The good catalytic performance of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs may be attributed to the specific characteristics of its nanostructure and the synergistic effect on the delivery of electrons between Ag NPs and Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. Furthermore, the as-prepared catalysts also show good activity for the reduction of other nitrobenzene analogs. The effect of solvent and reducing agent was also studied on the catalytic activity of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. Most importantly, the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag catalyst shows excellent recycling stabilities, which can be potentially applied in the fields of catalysis and green chemistry.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) have been received increasing attention due to their unique physical, chemical, and biological properties [1–5]. Notably, Ag NPs have been intensively studied for catalytic applications in various chemical reactions [6]. However, owing to the high surface energy, bare Ag NPs are usually unstable and tend to aggregate to form larger particles during catalytic processes, resulting in an obvious reduction of catalytic activity [7]. Moreover, the recovery of the Ag nanoparticle catalysts is difficult due to their small particle sizes. Thus, the stability and recovery of the Ag NPs are two crucial issues for their further applications. In this regard, immobilizing the Ag NPs on a solid support is proposed to be an effective route to improve their stability [8–12]. Among various supporting materials, magnetic materials exhibit distinct advantages, and they can not only prevent the aggregation of metal NPs, but also facilitate the recycle of nanocatalysts through magnetic separation. Jiang et al. [13] prepared the Ag@Fe3O4 nanospheres with core-shell structure, and used it as a recyclable catalyst for the reduction of p-nitrophenol (p-NP) in the presence of NaBH4. Baykal et al. [14] prepared the Fe3O4@Nico-Ag nanocomposite, and the obtained nanocatalysts also showed excellent recyclable and catalytic performance for the reduction of azo dyes. Nonetheless, the naked magnetic supports are easily to be oxidized and dissolved in an acidic medium during the recycling process. Therefore, a protective layer for the magnetic materials is required before loading Ag NPs.

Carbon dots (C-dots), a new member of the nanocarbon family, have attracted much attention because of their strong fluorescence, super conductivity and rapid electron transfer properties [15–17]. It is noteworthy that the abundant functional groups on the surface of C-dots as effective components which make it much easier to immobilize the metal NPs and further enhance the stability and catalytic activities of the original materials [18,19]. However, compared to other carbon structures, such as graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes, less attention has been paid to use C-dots as solid supports in catalysis design [20–23]. Thus, boosting the unique role of C-dots for catalyst design and a comparison with other conventional supports are of great interest.

In this work, we present a novel, green and low-cost ultrasonic method for the fabrication of multifunctional Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. The morphology, composition, structure, fluorescence and magnetic properties of the composite materials were studied. Besides, their catalytic properties toward the reduction of p-NA and CV dye were investigated in detail.

O-Phenylenediamine (o-PD, ≥98%), p-nitroaniline (p-NA), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) were purchased from Mclean reagent company. Iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O, analytical grade), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96%) and potassium nitrate (KNO3, ≥99%) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Reagent Company. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4 ≥96%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Energy Chemical. o-Nitroaniline (o-NA), m-nitroaniline (m-NA), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), m-nitrophenol (m-NP), p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and crystal violet (CV) were purchased from Chengdu cologne chemical reagent factory. All reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (18.2MWcm).

C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) [24]. Typically, 0.1g of o-PD was dissolved in ethanol (10mL) to form a clear solution. The mixture was then transferred into a 25mL Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The orange suspension was purified through a 0.22µm membrane filter and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48h to remove the unreacted reactants. Finally, the purified C-dots were diluted to 100mL with ultrapure water for further use.

The Fe3O4 NCs was prepared according to a previously reported method [25]. FeSO4·7H2O (0.7g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (80mL) and the solution was stirred by using a mechanical stirrer under Argon atmosphere. Subsequently, 10mL of KNO3 (2.0M) and 10mL of NaOH (1.0M) were added into the above solution, and the mixture was then heated in an oil bath with constant stirring at 90°C for 2h. After the reaction completed, the solution was cooled to room temperature naturally. The resulting black product was washed with ultrapure water and ethanol several times with centrifugation (10,000rpm), and then dried at 60°C for 6h in a vacuum oven.

In a typical experimental procedure, 0.01g of pristine Fe3O4 NCs was dispersed into the as prepared C-dot solution (15mL), and then the above mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 3h. The crude product was collected by magnetic separation and washed with ethanol and ultrapure water several times and finally dried at 60°C for 6h to obtain the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs.

Typically, 3mg of the prepared Fe3O4@C-dot NCs was dispersed in ultrapure water (15mL) by ultrasonication. Then, 1mL of AgNO3 (1mM) aqueous solution was slowly added into the above solution by ultrasonic agitation for 30min. Subsequently, 0.25mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 (0.1mM) solution was added dropwise for 1h with constant ultrasonic agitation. After ageing for another 30min, the resulting Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs was collected by a magnet, rinsed with ethanol and ultrapure water, and dried under vacuum at 60°C for 6h. For comparison, bare Ag NPs were also prepared by using a similar procedure without the addition of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the reduction of p-nitroaniline (p-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of the prepared nanocatalysts was evaluated by using aromatic nitro-compounds reduction reactions as a model system. In a typical catalysis reaction, 30μL of the prepared nanocatalyst (0.1mgmL−1) were added into a standard quartz cuvette containing 0.5mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.1M). Afterwards, 2mL of p-NA solution (0.1mM) was added into the above mixed solution, and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded immediately at room temperature.

To study the reusability of the catalyst, the nanocatalyst was recovered by using a magnet and then washed with water and ethanol several times for the next cycle. The catalytic reduction of other nitrobenzene compounds was conducted under the same condition of p-NA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the reduction of crystal violet (CV) dye
ce:section.ce:para: 0.25mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.05M) and 30μL of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs (0.1mgmL−1) were added into a standard quartz cuvette successively, and then 2.5mL of CV solution (0.05mM) was added to the mixed solution. The catalytic process was recorded with the UV–vis spectrophotometer at room temperature.

The morphology of the prepared C-dots, Fe3O4 NCs, Fe3O4@C-dot NCs and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs was characterized by using FESEM and TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 1, the prepared C-dots are spherical and well-dispersed. The particle size distribution obtained from TEM measurements shows that the diameter of the C-dots is about 0.4–1.7nm, with an average value of 1.0nm (inset of Fig. 1). Typical TEM and FESEM images of the obtained Fe3O4 NCs are shown in Fig. 2a–c and Fig. S1 (see the ESI†). It can be seen that the Fe3O4 NCs are cubic-like nanostructures and dispersed, with an average size of about 42nm. The interplanar distance calculated from the figure (Fig. 2c) is about 0.48nm, which is consistent with the (111) lattice plane of Fe3O4 NCs [26]. The morphology of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs is different compared with C-dots and Fe3O4 NCs. Fig. 2d–f displays the TEM images of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs with different magnifications, revealing that the Fe3O4 NCs are homogeneously dispersed and coated with low contrast continuous C-dot layers. The negatively charged C-dots (−1.51±0.8mV) (Fig. S2, see the ESI†) in the reaction solution can bind to the positively charged Fe3O4 NCs (10.55±0.6mV) (Fig. S3, see the ESI†) through electrostatic interaction. With the aid of ultrasonication, the C-dots will spontaneously coat on the surface of Fe3O4 NCs to achieve a minimum surface energy [27,28], resulting in the formation of core-shell nanostructures. In addition, the magnified TEM image was used to further investigate the detailed structure of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs (Fig. 2f). The image shows the single-crystallinity of the Fe3O4 NCs core and the amorphous nature of the C-dots shell. The size of the ultrathin C-dots shell around the Fe3O4 NCs is approximately 1.41nm. The morphology changes indicate that the C-dots are successfully assembled on the Fe3O4 NCs surface.

The functionalization of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs surface with Ag NPs was performed through a facile ultrasonic process. In general, the Ag ions can firmly load on the surface of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs through the ligand-assisted approach, and then reduced to Ag NPs by NaBH4 under ultrasonic waves. To evaluate the performance of the synthesis process, we have collected the residual solution by removing the solid product and determined the amount of unreacted Ag ions by ICP-AES. The result shows that only trace amount of Ag ions (0.13%) is detected in the residual solution, which further proves the efficiency of the synthesis procedure. Fig. 3 presents the TEM images of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. It reveals that the Ag NPs are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs, while maintaining the shape and the size of the core-shell Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. The average sizes of Ag NPs (Fig. S4, see the ESI†) are about 5.6nm. Furthermore, the HRTEM image shows that the interplanar spacing of the particle lattice is 0.23nm, which can be attributed to the (111) plane of Ag NPs, respectively (Fig. 3d) [29]. In addition, the C, H, Fe and Ag ratios of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs were obtained by elemental analysis and ICP-AES, and the values are listed in Table S4 (see the ESI†).

The TEM elemental mapping was also performed to reveal the element distribution in the composite materials. As shown in Fig. 4, the images show that the elements of C, N, O, Fe and Ag are evenly distributed in the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs, and the Ag NPs are uniformly dispersed on the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs surface, confirming the formation of the composite structure. The results are consistent with the EDX analysis (Fig. S5, see the ESI†).

XRD analysis provides evidence for the phase and crystal structure of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 5 displays the XRD patterns of C-dots, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C-dot and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. The diffraction pattern of the C-dots shows a broad peak centered at around 24°, which is related to the amorphous carbon (Fig. 5a) [30,31]. In addition, the diffraction peaks in curve b can be well-assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes of cubic structured Fe3O4 (JCPDS 00-019-0629) [26]. After coating with the C-dot layers, the characteristic peaks of the C-dots and Fe3O4 are observed in the resulting materials, demonstrating the formation of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs (Fig. 5c). The XRD patterns of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs also exhibit the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs (curve d). However, compared to Fe3O4@C-dot NCs, additional peaks (marked as *) at 2θ =38.2°, 44.5°, 64.7° and 77.1◦ are observed for the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of Ag (JCPDS 04-0783) [32], respectively. The above mentioned XRD results indicate that the Ag NPs are successfully immobilize on the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs surface.

Additionally, XPS was performed to study the surface chemical compositions of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. The XPS survey spectra confirm the existence of C, N, O, Fe and Ag elements in the composite materials, which are agreed well with the elemental mapping analysis ( Fig. 6a). Fig. 6b–e shows the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Fe 2p and Ag 3d, respectively. The C 1s spectra of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs display four peaks centered at 284.7, 285.5, 286.3 and 288.8eV corresponding to the C-C, C-N, C-O and O-C=O groups of the C-dots, respectively (Fig. 5b) [33]. Fig. 6c shows the N 1s spectra of the composite materials. It can be divided into three peaks located at 399.5eV, 400.5eV, and 401.5eV, which are attributed to the C-N-C, N-(C)3, and N-H bands, respectively [34]. The Fe 2p spectra in Fig. 6d show that Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks located at 711.2eV and 724.6eV, respectively, which correspond well with those of bulk Fe3O4 [35]. In addition, the high resolution Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 6e) shows characteristic peaks centered at 368.2 and 374.2eV, which can be ascribed to the Ag (0) state [36]. These results are consistent with the TEM and XRD analysis as described above.

Meanwhile, the FTIR spectra were recorded to identify the functional groups of the C-dots, Fe3O4@C-dot NCs and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs (Fig. S6, see the ESI†). For the C-dots, curve a, the peak located at 3414cm−1 can be ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of O-H and N-H [33]. In addition, three obvious absorption peaks at 1624, 1401 and 1247cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C=O (amide I band), C-N and C-O groups, indicating the presence of multifunctional groups on the surface of C-dots [37]. It is noteworthy that these functional groups on the surface of C-dots make it easier to immobilize the catalytic active species. Relative to the spectrum for the C-dots, new peaks appeared at lower frequencies for Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. These new peaks are attributed to the stretching vibration of Fe-O bonds in the Fe3O4 structure [32,38]. The characteristic peaks in the FT-IR spectrum (curve b) demonstrate that the C-dots successfully coated on the Fe3O4 NCs surface. In curve c, the absorption peaks of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs are similar to those of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. However, the intensity of the absorption peaks is weaker than that of Fe3O4@C-dot after being anchored with Ag NPs. The FTIR studies are in good agreement with the XPS investigation.

To gain a further insight into the interaction between metal NPs and C-dots, we examined the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the C-dots, Fe3O4@C-dot NCs and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs (Fig. S7, see the ESI†). Compared to the C-dots, there is an apparent decay in the PL intensity of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs. The obvious quenching of PL intensity proves that an ultrafast electron transfer process exists in the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag systems [23,39].

The magnetic property of magnetic materials was investigated with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 7, the magnetic hysteresis curves of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C-dot and Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs reveal that they possess ferromagnetic behaviors, and their magnetic saturation (Ms) values are 76.1, 72.0 and 51.2emug−1, respectively. The reduced Ms in the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs is possibly due to the presence of non-magnetic components [30,40]. The inset pattern demonstrates that the composite materials can be easily separated from the aqueous dispersion via an external magnetic field, which makes it possible for them to be recycled for reuse (the inset of Fig. 7).

The catalytic activity of the prepared nanocatalysts was evaluated by employing the reduction of p-NA in the presence of NaBH4. The reaction process was monitored by UV–vis spectrometry, as illustrated in Fig. 8. The mixture of the p-NA and NaBH4 aqueous solution exhibits an absorption maximum at 380nm (Fig. 8a). Nevertheless, there is little change in the absorption intensity at 380nm over time even after adding excess NaBH4 solution, indicating that the reduction reaction cannot proceed without catalyst. After the addition of the catalyst, the intensity of the absorption band at 380nm gradually decreases along with the increasing of reaction time (Fig. 8b–e). Meanwhile, two new peaks arise with the bleaching of the reaction solution at 238 and 305nm, which indicates the formation of p- phenylenediamine (p-PDA) (Fig. S8, see the ESI†) [41]. It is observed that the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs exhibit superior catalytic activity and finish the reaction in 126s, while more time is required for the other nanocatalyst samples. Furthermore, the apparent rate constants (kapp) are calculated for all nanocatalyst samples to make a better comparison. The concentration of NaBH4 is much higher than that of p-NA, thus ensuring that the reaction follows first-order kinetics [42]. Therefore, the reaction kinetics can be described as ln (Ct/C0) =-kappt, where kapp is the apparent kinetic rate constant (min−1), C0 is the initial concentration of p-AP, and Ct is the concentration of p-NA at time t. Linear relationships between ln (Ct/C0) and reaction time are displayed in Fig. 8f, which match well with the first order reaction kinetics. The kapp values indicate that the catalytic activities of these samples decrease in the following order: Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs (1.5478min−1) > bare Ag NPs (0.1798min−1) > Fe3O4@C-dot NCs (0.0128min−1) > C-dots (0.0030min−1). It can be concluded that the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4@C-dot based composite materials is enhanced compared to the corresponding bare Ag NPs (Fig. S9, see the ESI†), indicating the important role of the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs in the enhancement of the catalytic activity. The superior catalytic performance of the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs further confirms that the Fe3O4@C-dot NCs can serve as excellent support for metal NPs.

The catalytic activity of the as-prepared Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs was also investigated for the reduction of other nitrobenzene analogs. Fig. S10 (see the ESI†) shows the typical UV–vis absorption spectra of these nitrobenzene compounds during the reduction process, demonstrating that the reduction reaction indeed occurs with only a small amount of catalysts. Additionally, as listed in Table S1 (see the ESI†), the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs exhibit superior catalytic activity with excellent yields toward the nitrobenzene compounds regardless of the type and position of the substituents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanism for the reduction reaction
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the experimental results and the reported literatures [43–45], a plausible mechanism for the reduction reactions is proposed, as shown in Scheme 1. As described earlier, electron transfer from BH4 − to substrate molecules (p-NA) takes place when both of the species are adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst. In addition, the hydrogen atom, which is formed from the hydride after the electron transfer (ET) to the Ag NPs, attacks the substrate molecules to reduce it [46,47]. The superior catalytic activity of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs can be attributed to the following factors: (1) the small particle size of the Ag NPs, which provide a larger reaction area for reduction reaction; (2) Fe3O4@C-dot NCs support with large surface area (cubic-like structure), which can load large amount of Ag NPs and prevent their aggregation, providing more catalytic active sites and leading to the superior catalytic activity; (3) hydrophilic surface of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs, which can accelerate the catalytic reaction because they easily adsorb BH4 − donor on the surface of Ag NPs; (4) In addition, the good electron-transport properties of the C-dot shell facilitate the electron transfer and further enhance the catalytic rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison for the reduction of p-NA with different catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs as potential catalysts for the reduction of p-NA, the turnover frequency (TOF) is compared with that of other catalysts reported previously. As shown in Table 1, the TOF value can reach 2250h−1 for Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs, which is higher than most of reported catalysts [48–54]. On the other hand, the procedure for the fabrication of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs using an ultrasonic method is simple and environmentally friendly, which is another advantage compared to other catalyst systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of solvent and reducing agent on catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of p-NA in the methanol-NaBH4 reaction system was also carried out under the same conditions, and the results are listed in Table S2 (see the ESI†). It is interesting that the P-NA exhibits higher reactivity in methanol than that in aqueous solution, which indicates that the methanol-NaBH4 reaction system yielded much more amount of hydrogen than the water-NaBH4 system [43]. Moreover, we employed ammonia borane (AB) as a reducing agent and the reaction kinetics of the p-NA were also studied. It shows that the p-NA exhibits lower reactivity in the AB induced reduction reactions than in the NaBH4 systems, which is probably due to the slower hydrogen generation capacity of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag with the ammonia borane complex [52].

To examine the universality of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs as catalyst for other dyes, a reduction reaction for CV dye was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 9. The peak at 585nm decreases with the increase in absorption at 262nm, indicating the formation of leuco crystal violet (LCV) [49,55]. During the process of reduction reaction, the solution color changes from dark violet to colorless, which further confirms the catalytic activation of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs in the reduction of CV dyes. The rate constant of the CV reduction is calculated to be 2.6125min−1, which reveals that these catalysts show an effective catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison for the reduction of CV dye with different catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few years, various nanocatalysts and methods have been employed to remove the CV dyes [49,55–59]. Table S3 (see the ESI†) gives a comparison of the catalytic activities in our work with previous reports. Obviously, the catalytic activity of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs is higher than most of reported catalysts, even using a small amount of catalyst, which suggests that the as-prepared nanocomposites can be explored as promising catalyst for wastewater treatment.

For a heterogeneous catalyst, high durability and favorable cycle performance should be taken into account. The Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs can be easily separated from the reaction mixture by using a magnet and reused in the next run. The result in Fig. 10a shows that they can be successfully recycled for five successive cycles with no significant loss of activity, indicating that the catalysts are stable and efficient. In order to study the stability of the as-prepared catalysts, we performed TEM and XRD analysis of the catalyst after five catalytic cycles (Fig. 10b and c). The results reveal that the morphology of the catalyst is almost retained, and nearly no agglomeration is observed after the recyclability test. Additionally, the XRD pattern of the recycled catalyst shows a similar result to that of the fresh sample, which confirms the excellent stability of the as-prepared catalysts. On the other hand, a leaching test with the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs is also carried out under reaction conditions. The results reveal that only trace amounts of Ag and Fe elements are detected in the residual solution, which further proves the good stability of the catalyst (Table S4, see the ESI†).

In summary, we have demonstrated an easy and general ultrasonic method for the synthesis of Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag nanocubes. It is found that the Ag NPs are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of Fe3O4@C-dot NCs. The Fe3O4@C-dot NCs as a nanocarrier can not only significantly enhance the Ag NPs catalytic activity for the reduction of P-NA and CV dye, but also improve the catalyst ease of separation and recovery. The superior catalytic performance of the composites can be ascribed to their large specific surface areas, high reactivity and superior electronic conductivity. The above results indicate that the Fe3O4@C-dot/Ag NCs can be expected to be a promising candidate for various industrial catalytic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation for Fostering Talents in Basic Research of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. J1103307). The authors would like to thank the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21271094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2017.03.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
